questions.question: Which classical BFT assumptions do LLM agents violate?
options: None — PBFT transfers directly
Independent faults (same-model agents share failure modes), honest-node correctness (honest LLMs still hallucinate), and external ground truth (on ambiguous questions, consensus IS the answer)
Only the network-timing assumptions
The requirement that nodes use cryptographic signatures
questions.explanation: PBFT (Castro & Liskov 1999) tolerates f < n/3 arbitrary nodes — but it can't handle three honest agents sharing a hallucination because they share training data. Classical BFT is not wrong; it's incomplete for stochastic, correlated voters.

questions.question: What are the three LLM-specific attacks on consensus this lesson injects?
options: DDoS, phishing, ransomware
Byzantine lie (deliberate wrong answer), sycophantic conformity (aligning with whoever spoke last), and correlated-error monoculture (same-base-model agents hallucinating identically)
Jailbreak, injection, exfiltration
Overfitting, underfitting, drift
questions.explanation: Classical BFT handles the lie if f < n/3, but conformity passes every signature check, and a monoculture majority is "honestly" wrong — false majorities that no quorum machinery detects.

questions.question: How does CP-WBFT counter sycophantic conformity?
options: It bans agents from reading each other's answers
Votes are weighted by a confidence probe — conforming agents tend to report low confidence on positions they merely adopted, so their votes count less
It doubles the number of rounds
It removes the lowest-scoring agent each round
questions.explanation: Confidence-Probed Weighted BFT scales each vote by self-reported (or calibration-model) confidence, reporting +85.71% BFT improvement on complete graphs. Conformity correlates with weak conviction, which the probe exposes.

questions.question: Why does DecentLLMs use the geometric median for aggregation?
options: It is the fastest statistic to compute

The geometric median is robust to outliers and pulls toward the dense cluster rather than a model-biased average — countering both Byzantine lies and correlated errors, leaderlessly, with f < n/2 tolerance
It always equals the majority vote
It requires no evaluator agents
questions.explanation: Workers propose in parallel, evaluators score, and the geometric median of scored positions resists both a deliberate outlier and a biased cluster's pull — without any leader to compromise.

questions.question: What is the uncomfortable finding of "Can AI Agents Agree?" (2026)?
options: Agents agree too readily on everything

Even adversary-free scalar agreement fails >30% on many benchmarks, and a single deceptive persona can pull a Mixture-of-Agents consensus 40+ points off the honest baseline — BFT aligns outputs but can't certify they're right
Consensus is solved by using one big model
Disagreement only occurs in small models
questions.explanation: Heterogeneous ensembles disagree more (good: uncorrelated errors) but converge slower. The takeaway: consensus machinery is necessary but not sufficient — combine with verification roles, diversity, and evaluator agents.
